questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in an array?
questions.explanation: Arrays provide O(1) random access because elements are stored in contiguous memory. The address is calculated as: base + (index × element_size).

questions.question: A linked list provides O(1) access to any element by index.
questions.explanation: Linked lists require O(n) traversal to reach an arbitrary element since nodes aren't contiguous in memory. Only head (and tail in doubly-linked) access is O(1).

questions.question: What does this function return for input [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]?
questions.explanation: This builds a monotonic decreasing stack. Each element pops all smaller elements before it. After processing all elements: 3→[3], 1→[3,1], 4 pops 1,3→[4], 1→[4,1], 5 pops 1,4→[5], 9 pops 5→[9].

questions.question: Match each data structure to its best use case:
questions.explanation: Each structure excels at specific operations: HashMap for O(1) lookup, Heap for min/max extraction, Trie for prefix operations, Union-Find for connectivity queries.

questions.question: A balanced BST guarantees ___ time complexity for search, insert, and delete operations.
questions.explanation: Balanced BSTs (AVL, Red-Black) maintain height ≈ log₂(n), ensuring all operations traverse at most log(n) levels. Unbalanced BSTs can degrade to O(n) in the worst case.

questions.question: Sort these algorithms by average-case time complexity (fastest to slowest):
questions.explanation: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²). Note that these compare different operation types — search vs sort — but the complexity ordering holds.

questions.question: This binary search implementation has a subtle bug. Find it:
options: Integer overflow: (left + right) can overflow for large arrays
Off-by-one: should be while left < right (not <=)
Missing check: arr might not be sorted
The logic is actually correct — no bug exists
questions.explanation: In languages with fixed-size integers (Java, C), (left + right) can overflow when both are large. Fix: mid = left + (right - left) // 2. In Python this isn't an issue due to arbitrary-precision integers, but it's a classic bug in other languages.

questions.question: You need to find the K closest points to origin from 10 million points. Memory is limited to holding 1000 points. What approach?

scenario.context: Geographic data processing. K=1000. Points stream from a file that can't fit in memory.
constraints: Only 1000 points can be in memory at once
Must process in a single pass

options: Sort all points by distance (requires loading all into memory)
Max-heap of size K — maintain K closest seen so far
QuickSelect algorithm for O(n) average selection
questions.explanation: A max-heap of size K keeps the K smallest distances. For each new point: if distance < heap max, pop max and insert new point. O(n log K) time, O(K) space — perfect for streaming with limited memory.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of quicksort?
questions.explanation: Quicksort degrades to O(n²) when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (already sorted input with naive pivot selection). Randomized pivot selection makes this extremely unlikely in practice.

questions.question: A hash table has 1000 slots and 750 entries. Using the birthday paradox approximation, what's the expected number of collisions?
questions.formula: Expected collisions ≈ n - m × (1 - (1 - 1/m)^n) where n=entries, m=slots
questions.explanation: With load factor 0.75: expected occupied slots ≈ 1000 × (1 - (1-1/1000)^750) ≈ 1000 × 0.528 ≈ 528. Collisions = 750 - 528 ≈ 222-240. The exact formula gives ~236.

questions.question: You're building a real-time leaderboard for 50M users that updates every second. Which data structure?
questions.scenario: Mobile game leaderboard. Need: top-100 query in <10ms, rank lookup for any user in <10ms, score updates at 100K/second.
questions.explanation: Redis Sorted Sets are the industry standard for real-time leaderboards. ZADD for O(log n) updates, ZRANK for rank lookup, ZRANGE for top-K — all in-memory with <1ms latency at 50M entries.

questions.question: Breadth-First Search (BFS) always finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
questions.explanation: BFS explores all nodes at distance d before any at distance d+1. In unweighted graphs (all edges cost 1), the first time BFS reaches a node is guaranteed to be via the shortest path.

questions.question: Should tech companies use algorithm complexity as a primary hiring filter?
questions.scenario: FAANG companies reject ~95% of candidates based on DSA interviews. Critics argue this filters for 'puzzle ability' not engineering skill. Proponents say it tests foundational CS thinking.

options.label: Yes — DSA knowledge correlates with ability to write efficient production code
No — real engineering is about systems, collaboration, and domain knowledge
options.framework: Pragmatic — test what the job actually requires

options.label: Use as one signal among many (system design, past work, cultural fit)
Replace with paid trial periods that evaluate real work output

questions.explanation: Most industry leaders now advocate multi-dimensional evaluation. DSA tests pattern recognition but not software engineering judgment. The best hiring processes combine algorithmic thinking, system design, code review, and behavioral assessment.
